coredata.dc:title: Tunable Photoluminescence of Carbon Dots used for Homogeneous Glucose Sensing Assay
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Longyi
Dotzert, Michelle
Melling, C.W. James
Zhang, Jin
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) with tunable photoluminescence in a large range (80 nm) are produced by a microwave-assisted polyol method. The emission of CDs in aqueous can be adjusted by simply changing the precursors’ concentrations. The CDs with tunable photoluminescence have been further functionalized to develop a homogeneous glucose biosensor by incorporating with enzyme-conjugated graphene oxide. The fluorescence of CDs can be quenched through covalently interacting with the graphene oxide. The restored fluorescence is observed in the presence of glucose. The performance of the homogeneous sensing system has been evaluated by testing glucose levels in a range from 0.2 mM to 10 mM. Two linear relationships of the restored fluorescence intensity as a function of the concentration of glucose are obtained, i.e. 2 mM to 10 mM, and 0.2 mM to 1 mM, respectively. In addition, streptozotocin-induced diabetic rats are utilized to evaluate the performance of the designed sensor for testing diabetic rat’s plasma glucose. The results demonstrate that the homogeneous carbon-based biosensor can measure glucose in the blood and in other conditions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: tunable photoluminescence
carbon dots
homogeneous
glucose sensor
fluorescence resonance energy transfer
diabetic rat

Evaluation of the performance of the homogeneous glucose biosensing assay
Carbon dots used in glucose biosensor
Performance of the homogeneous glucose sensing assay

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Carbon-based nanostructured glucose sensor by using the fluorescence quenching mechanism.
(a) photo of different samples; (b) Photoluminescence of different CDs.
(a) TEM micrograph of CDs (Sample-3) made from the mole ratio of EG to carbon-based precursors at 100:1.0. (b) Photoluminescence of Sample-3 with different excitation from 340 nm to 500 nm.
UV–Vis absorbance spectra of carbon dots, Con A, andCon A-conjugating with CDs (CDs-ConA).
FTIR spectrum of (a) graphene oxide (GO), (b) chitosan conjugating GO, and (c) chitosan (CS).

Restored fluorescence intensity of the homogeneous glucose sensing systems to two ranges of glucose level. (a) and (b) show the restored PL of the sensing system when adding glucose in a range from 1 mM to 10 mM; (c) and (d) show the restored PL of the sensing system when adding glucose in the rage of 0.2 mM to 1 mM.

(a) The performance of the homogeneous glucose sensing system to the diluted rat blood glucose, 3 mM, 4.5 mM, 6 mM, 7.5 mM, 9 mM, and 10.5 mM. (b) The linear relationship of PL of the sensor as a concentration of the glucose in Rat blood (R2 = 0.994).
CDs made by the microwave-assisted polyol process with different molar ratios of EG to carbon-based precursors.

head.ce:title: Tunable Photoluminescence of Carbon Dots used for Homogeneous Glucose Sensing Assay

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemical and Biochemical Engineering, University of Western Ontario, London, Ontario, N6A 5B9, Canada
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemical and Biochemical Engineering, University of Western Ontario, London, Ontario, Canada, N6A 5B9

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Kinesiology, Faculty of Health Sciences, University of Western Ontario, London, Ontario, N6A 5B9, Canada
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Kinesiology, Faculty of Health Sciences, University of Western Ontario, London, Ontario, Canada, N6A 5B9

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Dept. of Chemical & Biochemical Engineering, University of Western Ontario, London ON, N6A 5B9, Canada

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) with tunable photoluminescence in a large range (80 nm) are produced by a microwave-assisted polyol method. The emission of CDs in aqueous can be adjusted by simply changing the precursors’ concentrations. The CDs with tunable photoluminescence have been further functionalized to develop a homogeneous glucose biosensor by incorporating with enzyme-conjugated graphene oxide. The fluorescence of CDs can be quenched through covalently interacting with the graphene oxide. The restored fluorescence is observed in the presence of glucose. The performance of the homogeneous sensing system has been evaluated by testing glucose levels in a range from 0.2 mM to 10 mM. Two linear relationships of the restored fluorescence intensity as a function of the concentration of glucose are obtained, i.e. 2 mM to 10 mM, and 0.2 mM to 1 mM, respectively. In addition, streptozotocin-induced diabetic rats are utilized to evaluate the performance of the designed sensor for testing diabetic rat’s plasma glucose. The results demonstrate that the homogeneous carbon-based biosensor can measure glucose in the blood and in other conditions.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) with an average diameter < 20 nm are a new class of fluorescence nanomaterials [1–4]. They show very attractive properties, e.g. good biocompatibility, chemical stability, facile surface modification, in particular, the multicolor photoluminescence [5–7]. However, the special photoluminescence mechanism of CDs is yet an open problem. To date, three possibilities have been proposed for their special photoluminescence behaviors; namely quantum size effect, surface state, and molecule state/carbon core state [5]. It is also noted that the fluorescence properties of CDs are related to synthetic methods. Various synthetic approaches have been explored for preparing CDs, including bottom-up techniques, e.g. electrochemical oxidation, and top-down methods, e.g. arc discharge, laser ablation [8–10].

Recently, microwave-assisted synthesis has been explored because of the strong interaction between carbon materials and microwave radiation [11–12]. Compared to other methods, e.g. hydrothermal process which often requires several hours or even days, high temperature, and high pressure, microwave represents an energy-saving and time-saving strategy [13–14]. The fluorescence of CDs made by microwave-assisted synthesis is either excitation-dependent or excitation-independent. To produce multicolor CDs, it often needs changing the reaction parameters including reaction temperature, reaction additives, pH, reaction time, etc [15–16]. Recently, Zhu and his co-workers demonstrate a simple way to produce CDs by varying the concentration of precursors [17]. However, the emission wavelength can be adjusted in a very narrow range (< 10 nm), therefore, the color of CDs solution did not change significantly.

Our previous studies demonstrate that the fluorescence properties of nanostructures highly depend on the surface states [18]. Herein, we develop a facile strategy to produce CDs with tunable photoluminescence by varying the ratio of carbon-based precursors to stabilizer agent. In our process, ethylene glycol (EG) acts as a reductive agent, and a stabilizer agent as well in the polyol synthesis [19]. Keeping the same concentration of ethylene glycol in the microwave-assisted process, we found that the CDs made from a higher concentration of carbon-based precursors, i.e. histidine and citric acid, show the red-shifted emission because of their high oxidative surface state. Previous studies indicate that higher oxidative surface state of CDs leads to more energy levels into their electronic structures, causing the broad and asymmetric emission from the blue to the red region [20–21].

On the other hand, the tunable luminescence of the produced CDs allows them to be nicely fit in the fluorescence sensing system. Glucose detection is important for various fields including disease diagnosis and management, food production, etc. People with diabetes need to measure glucose levels several times per day to manage chronic disease. Our group has developed different glucose sensors for detecting glucose in other body fluids, rather than in blood by using fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), bioluminescence resonance energy transfer (BRET) techniques [22–24].

FRET is a distance-dependent phenomenon. When the distance of the fluorescent donor and acceptor is smaller than 10 nm, the FRET can occur. Recently, the fluorescence quenching mechanism is applied for sensitivity detection [22–23]. Graphene oxide (GO) is a two-dimensional (2D) crystal structure made of carbon arranged in a hexagonal pattern with various oxygen functional groups on the surface, e.g. carboxyl, hydroxyl and epoxy groups [25]. GO has been demonstrated potential in the bio-sensing application as it is considered as a strong quenching element, and can be bioconjugated with different biomolecules [25–27]. Herein, we constructed a new homogeneous glucose sensor by incorporating CDs with special photoluminescence and GO acting as a FRET quencher. As depicted in Fig. 1 , concanavalin A (Con A), a glucose binding lectin protein [28], is conjugated onto the CDs (CDs-Con A); The surface of GO was modified with chitosan (CS) which can interact with Con A. When GO-CS mixed with CDs-Con A, the distance between CDs and GO is close to having the FRET quenching due to the interaction between CS and Con A In the presence of glucose, CDs-Con A is apart from GO-CS due to the stronger affinity between Con A and glucose than Con A and CS, resulting in the restore of the fluorescence of CDs.

Citric acid, L-histidine (H8000, ≥99%), dialysis tube (MWCO 1 kDa), Concanavalin A from Canavalia ensiformis (Jack bean), N-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride, N-Hydroxysuccinimide, Chitosan was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Graphite flake, natural, -325 mesh, 99.8% (metals basis) was purchased from VWR. Ethylene glycol was purchased from Caledon Laboratories Ltd. The water with 18.2 Ω resistivity was used throughout the experiment.

A microwave-assisted polyol method was adopted in the preparation of CDs [29–30]. Briefly, 100 mmol ethylene glycol, 0.5 mmol L-histidine, and 0.5 mmol citric acid was added in a vial and mixed well. Then the mixture was heated in a Panasonic 750 w Microwave Oven for 20 minutes. The deep brown color indicated the formation of CDs. The solutions were dialyzed against water and freeze-dried to obtain powder samples. The powder samples were stored in 4 °C. Different molar ratios of precursors were adjusted to produce four samples of CDs as shown in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the homogeneous assay for detecting glucose
ce:section.ce:para: 1 mg CDs (Sample 3) were firstly dissolved in 10 mL phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) (0.1 mg/mL). Following that, the carbodiimide crosslinker, i.e. 1 mg N-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-N'-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride) (EDC) and 3 mg

sulfo-N-hydroxysuccinimide (sulfo-NHS), was introduced into the CDs solution. After stirring for 1.5 hours at room temperature, the impurities and non-reactants were removed through the dialysis. Then 2 mg Con A (dissolved in 2 mL PBS) was added in CDs solution with the carbodiimide crosslinker. The mixture was stirred at 4 °C for 2 hours, and purified through the dialysis. On the other hand, the fluorescence quencher, graphene oxide (0.05 mg/mL) in 10 mL PBS was modified with 2 mg chitosan through EDC-crosslinking. Finally, 100 μL ConA-conjugated CDs (0.1 mg/mL) was mixed with 100 μL GO-CS solutions in different concentrations from 2 μg/mL to 10 μg/mL, respectively. The mixtures were incubated for 1 hour before fluorescence measurement.

The fluorescent emission spectra of carbon nanostructured samples were measured by fluorophotometry (QuantaMsterTM 30, PTI). The size and morphology of CDs were measured by transmission electron microscope (TEM, Philips CM-10 transmission electron microscope operating at 80 kV). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR, Bruker Vector 22 in the range of 400 cm-1-4000 cm-1) was used to obtain FTIR spectra. UV-vis spectra were obtained by Agilent Cary 60 UV-Vis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the performance of the homogeneous glucose biosensing assay
ce:section.ce:para: Two different concentration ranges of glucose level were studied, (1) the concentration range was from 0 to 1 mM with 0.2 mM interval to meet the glucose level in other body fluids, e.g. tears; (2) the other concentration range was from 1 to 10 mM with 2 mM interval to meet the glucose level in blood. of the volume of all glucose sample is 1 μL. In this study, the homogeneous glucose sensor was made of 10 μL CDs-Con A diluted by adding 90 μL water and 100 μL GO-CS (1 μg/mL) to measure the low concentration of glucose in the range of 0.0-1.0 mM. While the homogeneous glucose sensor was made of 100 μL CDs-Con A and 100 μL GO-CS (5 μg/mL) to measure the large concentration of glucose in the range of 1-10 mM; of the volume of all glucose sample is 1 μL. The fluorescence measurement on the level of glucose is normally taken after the mixture was incubated for 15 min. For the rat blood glucose sensing, the measurement is the same as the above steps.

Four male Sprague-Dawley rats (Charles River Laboratories, St. Constant, QC, Canada) were housed in a 12-h light/dark cycle room with humidity (50%) and temperature (21.5 °C) kept constant. Rats were given water and chow ad libitum and made diabetic with streptozotocin (STZ; Sigma-Aldrich, Oakville, ON, Canada). Intraperitoneal injections of STZ (20 mg/kg) dissolved in a citrate buffer (0.1 M, pH 4.5) were given over five consecutive days. Following the confirmation of diabetes (two blood glucose readings greater than 18 mM) subcutaneous insulin pellets were implanted in the abdominal region of rats. A blood sample was taken from the saphenous vein and measured blood glucose concentration by a glucometer (Freestyle Lite Blood Glucose Monitoring System, Abbott Diabetes Care Inc., Mississauga, Ontario).
Ethics approval was obtained through the University of Western Ontario Research Ethics Board, in accordance with the Canadian Council on Animal Care guidelines.

Table 1 lists the different samples of CDs synthesized by the microwave-assisted polyol process through adjusting the mole ratio of EG to C-based precursors from 100:0.2 to 100:2.0 where the two C-based precursors, i.e. L-histidine and citric acid, are at the equal mole No.

The photo of the above CDs samples at 0.1 mg/mL under a UV excitation light in the ambient environment is shown in Fig. 2 . The photoluminescence of CDs shows a significant red-shift from 430 nm to 548 nm under the excitation at 350 nm when the mole ratio of EG to C-based precursors increases from 100:0.2 to 100:2.0 as shown in Fig. 2. The photoluminescence (PL) of the CDs was measured by using fluorospectrometer. Interestingly, it is noted that the emission of samples becomes less dependent on the mole ratio of carbon-based precursors to ethylene glycol when it is larger than 100:2.0 (See the support document, Fig. S1). This could be interfered with by other factors, e.g. particle size, the excitation-depended properties of CDs.

Both dynamic light scattering (DLS) and TEM have been employed to study the particles. There is no significant difference in terms of particle size and size distribution to all samples (molar ratio of carbon precursor to EG ≤ 100 :2.0). Therefore, the red-shift emission of CDs is not dependent on the particle size in the microwave-assisted polyol process. A TEM micrograph of Sample-3 is shown in Fig. 3 a. The average particle size is measured at 20 ± 5 nm. As Sample 3 shows the emission at 512 nm which is a green light, and easy to be recognized by the naked eye, wee further studied the fluorescence emission of Sample-3 as a function of excitation. The photoluminescence of Sample-3 with different excitations. The typical excitation-dependent fluorescence is also observed (Fig. 3b). The emission wavelength increases from 500 to 570 when the excitation increases from 340 nm to 500 nm. The strongest fluorescence emission is entered at 500 nm under the excitation of 400 nm.

Though the mechanism of the tunable luminescence of CDs caused by the ratio of precursors is not yet fully understood. Our developed microwave-assisted polyol process offers an easy method to produce CDs with the tunable photoluminescence.
Due to the intensive and tunable photoluminescence of CDs, we further explored CDs to the homogeneous glucose sensors by incorporating GO acting as the fluorescent quencher.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon dots used in glucose biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Con A, a glucose-binding enzyme, is conjugated onto the CDs (Sample-3) through carbodiimide crosslinking process, i.e. N'-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N-ethylcarbodiimide (EDC) associated with N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS). The UV absorbance of CDs, Con A conjugating CDs, and Con A were measured. The UV absorption of CDs can be observed at 339 nm, while Con A has an absorbance peak at 280 nm as shown in Fig. 4 . The bioconjugation complex, CDs-Con A, shows two peaks in the UV-vis spectrum: one peak at 258 nm with 22 nm of blue-shift to the absorbance of pure Con A, and another peak at 342 nm with a slight red-shift as compared with that of CDs. The results of UV-vis indicate the successful bioconjugation of Con A to CDs.

On the other hand, two-dimensional nanostructure, GO, has been considered a strong fluorescence quencher due to its broad absorption (Fig. S2), which make GO an efficient fluorescent quenching element, and have been applied in FRET sensor [31].

The distance (< 10 nm) between the fluorescence element and the quenching element is vital for having efficient fluorescence energy transfer quenching performance [31–32]. Therefore, GO is modified with chitosan to be able to interact with Con A which conjugated onto CDs. The FTIR spectra of GO, chitosan (CS), and chitosan conjugating GO (GO-CS) are shown in Fig. 5 . The peaks at the 2981 cm-1 and 2884 cm-1 to the sample of GO-CS indicate the shift of -CH2- as compared with chitosan. The peak at 1714 cm-1 is distributed to the stretch of carboxyl group on GO, which is disappeared in the sample of GO-CS. Meanwhile, the peaks of 1621 cm-1 and 1542 cm-1 of chitosan are the stretches of the amine group which shift to 1631 cm-1 and 1567 cm-1 to sample of GO-CS. The results clearly show that CS is covalently bound on GO through carbodiimide crosslinking [33–34].

Various concentrations of GO-CS solutions were mixed and incubated with the solution of CDs-Con A to find out the optimal ratio of fluorescence donor and fluorescence quenching element (see supplementary document). In Fig. S3a, the fluorescence intensity of the system dramatically decreases to 27.8% when the concentration of GO-CS increases up to 10 μg/mL. The normalized fluorescence intensity at 500 nm is linearly decreased with increasing the centration of GO-CS (Fig. S3b); no linear relationship is observed when the concentration of GO-CS is beyond 10 μg/mL. Consequently, the configuration of the homogenous glucose biosensor is established by integrating the CDs with maximum fluorescence emission at 500 nm and the quencher, GO. The performance of the designed homogenous glucose biosensor was evaluated by testing the glucose level in two different ranges.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of the homogeneous glucose sensing assay
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 a is the PL spectra of the carbon-based homogeneous glucose sensing system responding to the concentration of glucose from 2 to 10 mM. Fig. 6b indicates that the restored fluorescence intensity linearly increases when the glucose concentration increases from 2 mM to 10 mM, which meets the range of blood glucose levels for diagnosis of hypoglycemia and hyperglycemia [35]. The other linear relationship of the restored fluorescence intensity of the homogeneous sensing system is found when the concentration of glucose increases from 0.2 mM to 1 mM as shown in Fig. 6c & d, which meets the general glucose level in body fluids, e.g. tears [36]. It is also noted that a low glucose level (<1 mM) may have a higher signal to noise ratio compared to the high concentration of glucose.

The performance of the homogeneous glucose sensor was further evaluated by using the blood of the streptozotocin-diabetic rat which is a well-accepted animal model for studying moderate hyperglycaemic patients with Type 1 diabetes mellitus under conventional insulin therapy [37]. Various rat blood glucose concentration of 3 mM, 4.5 mM, 6 mM, 7.5 mM, 9 mM, and 10.5 mM were prepared by dilution of the blood of streptozotocin-diabetic rat and measured by CDs-based sensor. The PL spectra of the homogeneous glucose sensor responding to the rat’s blood glucose are shown in Fig. 7 a. Good linearity between the restored fluorescence intensity vs. the concentration of plasma glucose is obtained (Fig. 7b). Compared to the detection of aqueous glucose solution (Fig. 6a and b), the homogeneous sensing assay responding to the blood samples shows lower fluorescent intensities. This might be caused by the other interferents presented in the blood, e.g. red cell, blood platelet, etc. These interferences can be also caused by auto-fluorescence and attenuated excitation light.

A microwave-assisted polyol process is developed to produce CDs with tunable photoluminescence in a large emission range from 430 nm to 548 nm. As compared to other reported studies on excitation dependent CDs, our process is able to produce tunable luminescence CDs under a certain excitation by simply adjusting the precursors' ratio. In addition, other methods for producing CDs often use various oxidative/reductive chemicals, whereas our method is facile and environmentally friendly. Although the mechanism is not fully revealed, the CDs are suitable for the application in fluorescence sensing system due to this special tunable luminescence. In this study, we herein demonstrate the development of homogeneous glucose sensors by incorporating CDs and GO. The chitosan coupling GO can be replaced by glucose to bind onto Con A conjugated on CDs. Without glucose, GO can significantly quench the fluorescence intensity of CDs; in the presence of glucose, the fluorescence intensity is restored. Two linear relationships of the restored fluorescence intensity of the homogeneous sensor vs. glucose level were studied; one is the glucose level in the ranges of 2 mM to 10 mM, which is suitable for blood glucose test for management of diabetes, the other one is the glucose level from 0.2 mM to 1 mM, which can be performed with other body fluids, tear. Different concentrations of blood glucose of streptozotocin-diabetic rats were measured by using our homogeneous carbon-based biosensor, the linear relationship of the fluorescence intensity vs. blood glucose level is obtained. The flexibility and broad sensing range of this carbon-based nanostructured sensor can be used in glucose detection at various circumstances.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful for the financial support from the Canada Innovation Fund-Leaders Opportunity Fund. L. Y. Chen and J. Zhang conceived and designed the experiments. L. Y. Chen performed major parts of experiments and analyzed the results. M. Dotzert and J. Melling were involved in the animal study. The manuscript was written through the contributions of all authors. All authors have approved the final version of the manuscript.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bej.2020.107580.

ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Noble, A model of poorly controlled type 1 diabetes mellitus and its treatment with aerobic exercise training, Diabetes & Metabolism. 39 (2013) 226–235. doi:10.1016/j.diabet.2013.02.004.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dr. Longyi Chen earned his PhD degree in 2018 from the Department of Chemical and Biochemical Engineering, University of Western Ontario. He is currently a post-doctoral fellow.
Miss Michelle Dotzert is currently pursuing her PhD at the School of Kinesiology, Faculty of Health Sciences, University of Western Ontario.

Prof. C.W. James Melling is a tenured Associate Professor at the School of Kinesiology, Faculty of Health Sciences, University of Western Ontario (Western University). Dr. Melling earned his PhD from Western University. His research contribution to the impact of exercise modality in an experimental Type 1 Diabetic rat model was featured in the Canadian Journal of Diabetes highlighting the novelty of the work and demonstrating how research can be advanced from the benchwork to clinical practice.

Prof. Jin Zhang is a tenured Associate Professor in the Department of Chemical and Biochemical Engineering in the University of Western Ontario (Western University), Canada. Dr. Zhang earned her PhD degree from the National University of Singapore. Her research activities are related to the development of nanocomposites with enhanced magnetic, optical, electric, and mechanical properties. Her expertise lies in the nanobiotechnology, surface engineering, and interface between multifunctional nanostructures and biological systems. Dr. Zhang has received many awards, including the Grand Challenges Canada-Canadian Rising Stars in Global Health (2011), Early Research Award of Ontario (2014), Outstanding Mid-Career Achievements in Nanoscience and Nanotechnology in Ontario (2017), etc.
